questions.question: Roughly how many seconds are in a day, the number you divide by to turn requests/day into average QPS?
options: About 3,600
About 86,400
About 604,800
questions.explanation: A day has 86,400 seconds (24 x 60 x 60). For fast mental math many engineers round it to ~100,000 (10^5). 3,600 is an hour; 604,800 is a week.

questions.question: Why is peak QPS, not average QPS, used for capacity planning?
options: Average QPS is impossible to calculate
Traffic is bursty, so the system must survive the peak, which is often several times the average
Peak QPS is always exactly double the average
Average QPS only applies to storage, not requests
questions.explanation: Real traffic is never flat — it spikes at busy hours. If you provision only for the average, the system falls over at peak. Peaks are commonly 2-10x the average, captured by a 'peak factor'.

questions.question: An app has 10M DAU making 20 requests each per day. What is the approximate average QPS?
options: About 230 QPS
About 2,300 QPS
About 23,000 QPS
About 200,000 QPS
questions.explanation: 10M x 20 = 200M requests/day. Divided by 86,400 seconds gives about 2,300 average QPS. Apply a 5x peak factor and you get ~11,500 peak QPS — past a single server, so you need a load balancer.

questions.question: Which ordering of access latencies (fastest to slowest) is correct?
options: Cross-continent round trip < SSD read < RAM read < L1 cache
L1 cache < RAM read < SSD read < cross-continent round trip
RAM read < L1 cache < cross-continent round trip < SSD read
SSD read < RAM read < L1 cache < cross-continent round trip
questions.explanation: L1 cache (~1ns) < main memory (~100ns) < SSD random read (~100us) < cross-continent round trip (~150ms). Memory is ~100x faster than SSD, and a cross-continent trip dwarfs everything — which is exactly why caching and CDNs exist.

questions.question: Your estimate assumes 500 KB per photo but the real average is 800 KB. Why is the estimate still useful?
options: It isn't — you must redo it with the exact number
The order of magnitude (petabytes/year, not gigabytes) is unchanged, so the design conclusion holds
Because 800 KB rounds down to 500 KB
Because storage cost is fixed regardless of size
questions.explanation: Back-of-the-envelope math targets order of magnitude, not precision. A 1.6x error still lands you at 'petabytes per year', so the conclusion (object storage + CDN, not a DB blob column) is the same. Round aggressively and move on.
